exercise.name: Exercise
exercise.scope:
Cardiorespiratory fitness, resistance/strength, mobility/balance. The second-largest modifiable cluster after substances ‚Äî and unlike substances, almost entirely a "do more" rather than "stop" lever.

- In: aerobic/cardiorespiratory training and measured fitness (VO2max / METs); resistance/strength training (incl. grip strength as a marker); mobility / balance / flexibility training; dose-response and minimum effective dose; detraining/retraining (reversibility); falls & fracture prevention via the exercise route.
- Out (and where it lives instead): post-exercise recovery & sleep ‚Üí `sleep`; protein and dietary intake to support training ‚Üí `nutrition-metabolic`; sedentary-behavior metabolic effects (sitting, glucose) ‚Üí noted here only where they share a mortality endpoint, otherwise `nutrition-metabolic`; orthopedic injury management / DEXA & screening decisions ‚Üí `medical-screening` (the fracture *risk reduction* claim stays here); cognitive/mood benefits of exercise ‚Üí ticked in `neurological`/`stress-social` but the mortality/healthspan claims stay here.

claims.text: Cardiorespiratory fitness (CRF) is the single strongest exercise-related mortality predictor ‚Äî high vs low fitness nearly halves all-cause mortality
claims.effect: High-vs-low CRF HR **0.47** (0.39‚Äì0.56) & 0.59 (0.52‚Äì0.66). Per **+1 MET**: all-cause HR 0.83‚Äì0.89 (‚âà11‚Äì17% ‚Üì; Kodama RR 0.87 [0.84‚Äì0.90]); CHD/CVD events RR 0.85 (0.82‚Äì0.88) per MET. Low fit (<7.9 METs) vs high (‚â•10.9): all-cause RR **1.70** (1.51‚Äì1.92), CVD 1.56 (1.39‚Äì1.75)
claims.reversibility: SLOW (CRF is trainable & lost with detraining)

claims.text: Most of the aerobic benefit is bought going from SEDENTARY to ~meeting guidelines ‚Äî a strong 80/20 / minimum-effective-dose effect
claims.effect: ~150 min/wk moderate activity **alone** ‚Üí all-cause HR **0.79‚Äì0.81** (‚âà19‚Äì21% ‚Üì vs sedentary); continued but diminishing returns to ~300 min/wk; **no U-shape / no harm** observed at these doses

claims.text: Vigorous-activity dose-response is curvilinear and flattens early; a *mix* of intensities beats all-out vigorous
claims.effect: All-cause HR **0.78** (0.76‚Äì0.79) at ~180 min/wk VPA vs light-moderate; modest gains past 180; **reduced** benefit >450 min/wk (sparse, weak ‚Äî see #17). Intensity mix: optimal ~37.5% vigorous HR 0.90 < 100%-vigorous HR 0.95 (CI crosses 1.0)

claims.text: Activity *pattern* doesn't matter once weekly volume is met ‚Äî "weekend warrior" ‚âà spread-out
claims.effect: All-cause: weekend-warrior HR **0.77** (0.68‚Äì0.87) vs regularly-active 0.70 (0.63‚Äì0.78); CVD WW 0.80 ‚âà RA 0.79. **No significant difference** between patterns

claims.text: Resistance training lowers mortality INDEPENDENTLY of aerobic activity, at a small effective dose
claims.effect: ~60‚Äì119 min/wk (adjusted for aerobic): all-cause HR **0.87** (0.81‚Äì0.95) @ 90‚Äì119 min; CVD HR 0.81 (0.67‚Äì0.97); neuro-disease HR 0.73 (0.58‚Äì0.92). **Plateau** above ~120 min/wk. The competing "strength-alone gives NO benefit" claim was **refuted** (see #15)
claims.reversibility: SLOW (muscle is trainable & lost with detraining)

claims.text: Combining aerobic + resistance gives the largest mortality reduction ‚Äî but the increment over high-aerobic-alone is small
claims.effect: Combined all-cause HR **0.50‚Äì0.55** vs sedentary (‚âà45‚Äì50% ‚Üì): Zhang 0.55, L√≥pez-Bueno 0.50. High-aerobic alone already 0.53‚Äì0.58 ‚Äî combo adds a little

claims.text: Grip strength is a robust, continuous all-cause & CVD mortality PREDICTOR ‚Äî a frailty biomarker, **not** proof that grip training extends life
claims.effect: Lowest-vs-highest HR **1.41** (1.30‚Äì1.52) all-cause, 1.63 (1.36‚Äì1.96) CVD; per **‚àí5 kg** HR 1.16 (1.12‚Äì1.20); per **‚àí1 SD** HR 1.30 all-cause, 1.75 CVD

claims.text: Simple balance/functional tests strongly predict mortality & disability ‚Äî prognostic markers, **not** proof balance training cuts mortality
claims.effect: Failing a **10-s one-leg stance** (ages 51‚Äì75): all-cause HR **1.84** (1.23‚Äì2.78), adjusted. **SPPB** 0‚Äì3 vs 10‚Äì12: OR **3.25** (2.86‚Äì3.79); 4‚Äì6 OR 2.14; 7‚Äì9 OR 1.50

claims.text: **[RCT]** Balance/functional ("postural-control") training is the single most effective modality for preventing FALLS ‚Äî and this is causal, not just predictive
claims.effect: Rate of falls **RaR 0.76** (0.70‚Äì0.81, Cochrane HIGH); number of fallers supervised **RR 0.82** (0.75‚Äì0.91), home-based 0.66 (0.49‚Äì0.88, sparse). Balance+resistance ‚Üí RaR **0.66** (0.50‚Äì0.88). Absolute: ~5‚Äì8 fewer fallers, 14‚Äì23 fewer falls, 2‚Äì3 fewer injured per 100 people

claims.text: **[RCT]** What does NOT prevent falls: walking-only, flexibility-only, and pure resistance/endurance programs show no moderate-certainty benefit; combined/multifactorial works but less than pure balance training
claims.effect: Combined/multifactorial RR **0.88‚Äì0.93** (ranks below postural-control); walking/flexibility/resistance-or-endurance alone: no moderate-certainty effect (home multifactorial RR 0.97). Multi-component is best for the balance-test *surrogate* (unipedal-stance g 0.97)

claims.text: **[RCT]** Exercise reduces FRACTURES ~25‚Äì33% ‚Äî fall-related, low-trauma, and major osteoporotic
claims.effect: Fall-related fracture **RR 0.74** (0.59‚Äì0.92); low-trauma overall IR 0.67 (0.51‚Äì0.87); major osteoporotic IR 0.69 / RR 0.75. Three independent meta-analyses converge, low heterogeneity (I¬≤<13%)

claims.text: **[RCT]** Supervised programs roughly DOUBLE the anti-fracture effect of unsupervised ‚Äî but no trial is powered for fracture as a primary endpoint
claims.effect: Supervised fracture IR **0.44** (0.27‚Äì0.73) vs unsupervised IR 0.83 (NS, p=0.24). **No RCT adequately powered for fracture as a primary outcome** ‚Üí BMD is the accepted surrogate; supervision effect is confounded by intensity/adherence

claims.text: **[RCT]** Only HIGH-intensity resistance + impact training (HiRIT) BUILDS bone; low/moderate intensity at best halts loss ‚Äî and the hip barely responds densitometrically
claims.effect: HiRIT (‚â•80% 1RM / ground-reaction-force ‚â•4√ó BW): **lumbar spine +2‚Äì4%** over 8 mo (LIFTMOR +2.9% vs ‚àí1.2% control; MEDEX-OP 1.9% vs 0.1%). Femoral neck +0.3% vs ‚àí2.0% (maintenance, structural not densitometric). HiRIT>moderate SMD 2.37 but very-low GRADE (I¬≤=98%)

claims.text: **[RCT/obs-extension]** Bone (and strength) gains are REVERSIBLE ‚Äî detraining returns BMD toward baseline within ~12 mo; continued training sustains and extends gains
claims.effect: 3-yr LIFTMOR extension: continuers **+8.63%** LS BMD vs discontinuers +2.18% (p=0.042); "BMD can return to baseline after ~12 months detraining." This is the SLOW-reversibility column made concrete: benefits require maintenance

claims.text: "Muscle-strengthening alone (without aerobic) confers NO mortality benefit" (HR 0.94, NS) ‚Äî TESTED and REFUTED here (0‚Äì3)
claims.effect: One JAMA-cohort framing; refuted in adversarial verification ‚Üí the independent RT mortality benefit (#5) stands. Don't cite the null

claims.text: The optimal resistance-training DOSE is unsettled across the two best mortality cohorts
claims.effect: Largest cohort shows a **plateau** at 90‚Äì119 min/wk; an older 16-cohort meta found a **J-curve peaking 30‚Äì60 min/wk** ("any amount" RR 0.85). Both agree the effective dose is *small*

claims.text: Extreme aerobic volume (>450 min/wk vigorous) showing "reduced benefit" is a weak signal
claims.effect: Rests on sparse, author-flagged unstable data. No evidence of *harm* ‚Äî read as "returns flatten," not "exercise becomes dangerous"

claims.text: Over-large intervention effect estimates that did NOT survive verification ‚Äî do not cite
claims.effect: All refuted 0‚Äì3: falls "OR 0.32 / 68% reduction"; resistance‚Üífemoral-neck BMD "SMD 0.89" & lumbar "SMD 0.88"; "Tai Chi has the most outcomes with benefit"; "fractures reduced only in >12-mo trials." Use the conservative figures in #9‚Äì#13 instead

exercise.dose:
**The 80/20:** unlike substances (where the win is total cessation), exercise's win is **getting off zero**. The dose-response is steepest at the bottom; the first ~150 min/week of moderate activity buys most of the *mortality* benefit, and returns flatten well before "elite" volumes. The falls/fracture/bone benefits, by contrast, are **modality-specific** ‚Äî you have to do the *right kind*, not just more.

- **Aerobic ‚Äî minimum effective dose:** ~150 min/wk moderate (or ~75 min vigorous) buys ‚âà19‚Äì21% lower all-cause mortality vs sedentary. Returns flatten by ~300 min/wk moderate / ~180 min/wk vigorous; the high-volume "reversal" (#17) is weak. Pattern is irrelevant ‚Äî weekend-warrior = spread-out (#4).
- **Intensity:** measured **CRF** is the strongest predictor (each +1 MET ‚âà ‚àí11‚Äì17% mortality). A *mix* of intensities (~30‚Äì60% vigorous) edges out all-out vigorous (#3).
- **Resistance ‚Äî small effective dose for mortality:** ~60‚Äì119 min/wk (‚âà2 sessions) captures the independent benefit; **plateaus** above ~120 min/wk (#5). For *bone*, though, intensity is the lever, not volume: only **HiRIT** (heavy, ‚â•80% 1RM, or high-impact) builds lumbar-spine BMD; moderate lifting maintains but doesn't build (#13).
- **Balance ‚Äî the falls dose:** supervised, **>3-month, ‚â•2√ó/week balance/functional training** (Otago-style, or balance+resistance) is what moves falls and fractures. Walking, stretching, or lifting *alone* do **not** prevent falls (#10). The benefit is not banked ‚Äî it fades when you stop (#14, and entirely so in frail aged-care populations).
- **The best single prescription:** aerobic (for mortality) + heavy resistance/impact (for bone & strength) + a balance/functional component (for falls). The three modalities buy *different* endpoints ‚Äî they're not interchangeable.
exercise.actions:
- [ ] **Get off zero first.** If sedentary, reach ~150 min/week of moderate aerobic activity (brisk walk, zone-2). Buys ~1/5 of all-cause mortality risk ‚Äî most of exercise's longevity benefit.
- [ ] **Build cardiorespiratory fitness as the primary longevity target.** Push some volume to higher intensity (a ~30‚Äì60% vigorous mix beats all-easy or all-hard); aim to climb MET categories over time.
- [ ] **Resistance train ~2√ó/week.** For *mortality* ~60‚Äì120 min/wk is enough (plateaus after). For *bone*, make some of it **heavy/high-intensity (HiRIT)** ‚Äî moderate loads maintain bone but don't build it.
- [ ] **Add a dedicated balance/functional component** (single-leg work, Otago-style, or tai chi within a balance program). This is the *causal, RCT-proven* lever for falls (RaR ~0.76) and fall-related fractures (~‚àí25‚Äì33%) ‚Äî and walking/stretching/lifting alone won't deliver it.
- [ ] **Treat it as maintenance, not a deposit.** Fitness, strength, and bone gains reverse with detraining over months ‚Äî the benefit requires *continuing*, which is why this is a lifelong habit, not a project.
- [ ] **Don't over-optimize scheduling.** Weekend-warrior is fine for the aerobic/mortality piece ‚Äî total weekly volume is what matters.
exercise.caveats:
- **Two different evidence tiers, don't blend them.** The *mortality/longevity* numbers (#1‚Äì#8) are OBSERVATIONAL ‚Äî reverse causation (subclinically ill people are less fit/weaker) and healthy-adherent bias (exercisers smoke less, eat better, higher SES) inflate them; read as upper bounds. The *falls/fracture/BMD* numbers (#9‚Äì#14) are RCT INTERVENTION evidence ‚Äî genuinely causal, the strongest tier in this project ‚Äî but fracture is a secondary/underpowered endpoint (#12) and BMD is a surrogate for fracture.
- **Surrogate vs hard endpoint:** grip strength (#7) and balance tests (#8) *predict* mortality but aren't proof that training them lowers mortality. The case that exercise extends life rests on the cohort data (#1‚Äì#6); the case that it prevents *falls/fractures* rests on the RCTs (#9‚Äì#11). Keep those two claims separate ‚Äî exercise's proven *causal* wins are falls/fracture/bone/strength, while its longevity effect, though large, is associational.
- **Modality specificity is real:** "exercise" is not one thing. Aerobic buys mortality; heavy resistance buys bone & strength; balance buys falls. Walking is great for #1‚Äì#4 and useless for falls (#10). Don't let a single word hide three different prescriptions.
- **Reversibility is SLOW, not irreversible** ‚Äî the key contrast with substances. Fitness, muscle, and bone gains are lost with detraining (BMD toward baseline in ~12 mo, #14) and regained with retraining. Benefits require maintenance; a fit past doesn't bank permanent protection.
- **Population fit:** the falls/fracture and balance-predictor evidence comes from older adults (mean ages ~70s) ‚Äî most relevant as you age. In *frail/residential* populations the falls benefit holds during training but **disappears once training stops** (high-certainty), so continuity matters even more there. For a healthy mid-life adult, the aerobic/CRF and resistance claims carry the weight; balance/bone work is preventive investment for later.
- **The combined-training edge over high-aerobic-alone is small for *mortality*** (#6) ‚Äî don't oversell "you must lift to live longer." Lift for strength, bone, falls, and function; the marginal mortality gain over being aerobically fit is modest.
exercise.openQuestions:
*(also copied to `synthesis/open-questions.md`)*
- **Sarcopenia / muscle-mass & strength intervention magnitudes** ‚Äî the targeted run nailed falls/fracture/BMD but the resistance-training effect sizes on *muscle mass, strength, gait speed, chair-stand* in sarcopenic/older adults did **not** survive verification as clean numbers. Direction is certain (RT builds muscle/strength); the pooled magnitudes are still un-pinned. Lower priority ‚Äî doesn't change the action ("lift heavy, ~2√ó/wk").
- **Muscle/strength detraining-retraining kinetics** ‚Äî only *bone* detraining was quantified (~12 mo to baseline). How fast strength/muscle are lost and regained in older adults is still unquantified here. Physiologically SLOW; specifics open.
- **Resistance dose optimum** ‚Äî plateau (~90‚Äì119 min/wk) vs J-curve (peak 30‚Äì60 min/wk) disagreement (#16). Both point to "~2√ó/wk is enough," so low decision-relevance.
- **Anti-fracture mechanism split** ‚Äî how much of exercise's fracture reduction is via *falls* (mechanical) vs *BMD/bone-quality*? No adequately-powered primary-endpoint trial separates them (#12). Academic, not action-relevant.
- *(Resolved this session: whether training ‚Äî not just fitness ‚Äî causally reduces falls & fractures. YES, RCT-grade, #9‚Äì#11.)*

medical-screening.name: Medical & Screening
medical-screening.scope:
Vaccination, age-appropriate cancer screening, pharmacologic primary prevention, biomarker monitoring. The "catch-it-early / prevent-with-a-pill" lever ‚Äî where the key tension is disease-specific vs all-cause mortality, overdiagnosis, and screening-hype.

- In: age-appropriate cancer screening (colorectal, breast, cervical, lung-LDCT, prostate-PSA) ‚Äî mortality benefit, NNS, overdiagnosis; cardiovascular & metabolic PHARMACOLOGIC primary prevention (statins, antihypertensives, aspirin, lipid/BP targets, coronary-artery-calcium scoring, diabetes/HbA1c screening) ‚Äî routed here from `nutrition-metabolic`; adult VACCINATION (influenza, shingles/Shingrix, pneumococcal, RSV, COVID); biomarker monitoring & the executive-physical / full-body-MRI / multi-cancer-early-detection (Galleri) hype; polypharmacy / deprescribing; baseline physician relationship.
- Out (and where it lives instead): the behavioral levers that reduce the NEED for screening/drugs (diet ‚Üí `nutrition-metabolic`, exercise ‚Üí `exercise`, smoking ‚Üí `substances`); disease *treatment* beyond primary prevention (out of project scope); dental screening ‚Üí `oral-sensory`; skin-cancer screening & dermatology ‚Üí noted here only if it shares a mortality endpoint, else `sun-skin`; hearing/vision screening ‚Üí `oral-sensory`/sensory.

claims.text: **[RCT] Colorectal screening** has the best benefit-harm ratio ‚Äî but even colonoscopy's ITT all-cause mortality was null
claims.effect: NordICC colonoscopy: CRC **incidence RR 0.82** (NNS 455) but CRC-mortality RR 0.90 (NS) & **all-cause RR 0.99 (null)** at 10yr ITT (~42% screened; per-protocol larger). FOBT: CRC-mortality RR 0.86 (14%‚Üì), all-cause null

claims.text: **[RCT] Lung LDCT** (heavy smokers only) is the rare screen with a small ALL-CAUSE benefit
claims.effect: NLST lung-cancer mortality **‚àí20%** + all-cause **IRR 0.93** (significant, but entirely attributable to lung-cancer deaths; fragile); NELSON ‚àí24% (men). Cost: ~26%/round false-positive, PPV ~3.5%

claims.text: **Cervical (HPV/cytology) screening** is among the strongest by magnitude ‚Äî but the mortality figure is modeled
claims.effect: Primary hrHPV q5y modeled **~97% lower** cervical-cancer death; USPSTF Grade A. Real-world programs show ~60‚Äì90% incidence reductions. No all-cause RCT (a single-screen Indian RCT claim was refuted)

claims.text: **The all-cause mortality critique** (Bretthauer 2023): common cancer screens have NOT been shown to extend overall life
claims.effect: Lifetime gained: **only sigmoidoscopy significant (110 days)**; mammography 0, PSA 37, colonoscopy 37, FOBT 0, lung-CT 107 ‚Äî all **NS**. (Counter: individual trials underpowered for all-cause; "NS" ‚âÝ "zero")

claims.text: Screening's apparent *survival* benefits are inflated by lead-time & length bias; overdiagnosis is real
claims.effect: Why mortality (not survival) is the endpoint. Overdiagnosis: breast DCIS up to **37% non-progressive** (prevalence screen); indolent prostate (PSA) substantial. Healthy-screenee confounding live

claims.text: **[RCT] Statins (primary prevention)** reduce vascular events robustly; all-cause mortality benefit is real but modest
claims.effect: **~20% fewer major vascular events per 1 mmol/L LDL** (RR 0.81), NNT ~91 over 5yr (low-risk); all-cause RR ~0.90 (modest; the strong "no mortality benefit" claim was **refuted**). Relative benefit constant across baseline risk ‚Üí absolute benefit scales with risk

claims.text: Statin muscle symptoms are **largely a nocebo effect**
claims.effect: SAMSON/StatinWISE: muscle-symptom scores on statin ‚âà placebo (1.68 vs 1.85, NS). "Statin intolerance" is mostly symptom *attribution*, not pharmacology. (Real myopathy rare; new diabetes ~0.2%/yr)

claims.text: **[RCT] Blood-pressure lowering** is the prevention drug class with the clearest all-cause mortality benefit ‚Äî but age-dependent
claims.effect: Per 5 mmHg systolic: CV-events HR **0.82** (<55yr) ‚Üí 0.91 (55‚Äì84) ‚Üí **NS at 85+**. **SPRINT** intensive (<120): all-cause **HR 0.73** (NNT 90), CV HR 0.75 (NNT 61); harm: AKI HR 1.64 (NNH ~67). Only class with a mortality signal in 2025 NMA

claims.text: **[RCT] Shingrix/RZV** is one of the most effective adult vaccines ‚Äî strong, durable
claims.effect: Herpes-zoster efficacy **97.2%** (50+) / 89.8% (70+); postherpetic neuralgia ~89‚Äì91%; durable to **~11 years**. Disease-incidence endpoint, no healthy-vaccinee confounding (placebo-controlled)

claims.text: **[RCT] RSV vaccines** (Arexvy/Abrysvo/mResvia, 60+) are genuinely high-efficacy against severe RSV ‚Äî but wane
claims.effect: ~**73‚Äì83%** vs RSV hospitalization (real-world TND); mResvia ~80% vs symptomatic RSV early. **Wanes** 80%‚Üí56%(12mo)‚Üí34%(18mo) ‚Üí timing/revax matters

claims.text: **[RCT] Pneumococcal PCV13** works against vaccine-type disease but shows NO mortality benefit
claims.effect: CAPiTA (n=84,496): **45.6%** vs vaccine-type pneumonia, **75%** vs vaccine-type IPD; but infection-death VE 14.7% (NS); all-cause pneumonia benefit much weaker

claims.text: **Aspirin for primary prevention: the REVERSAL** ‚Äî net-neutral, a "less is more" lesson
claims.effect: ARRIVE/ASCEND/ASPREE: small CV-event reduction **offset by major bleeding**; **no all-cause mortality benefit** (RR ~0.98; ASPREE showed a *non-significant mortality increase* in healthy elderly). Guidelines reversed. Don't take routinely without specific indication

claims.text: **Influenza vaccine: the healthy-vaccinee calibration lesson** ‚Äî observational mortality claims are confounded fiction
claims.effect: Observational "~47‚Äì50% all-cause winter mortality reduction" is **mathematically impossible** (flu <10% of deaths); the "effect" appears *outside* flu season (proves confounding); 83% of obs studies high-bias. **Cochrane RCT all-cause RR 1.02 (null)**; real benefit is modest (lab-confirmed flu ‚Üì, hospitalization OR ~0.71). Still worth getting ‚Äî just not a mortality lever

claims.text: **Coronary-artery-calcium (CAC) & diabetes screening** are risk-STRATIFIERS, not life-savers
claims.effect: CAC reclassifies risk (CAC=0 ‚Üí low; high ‚Üí up) but **no RCT** shows CAC-guided treatment saves lives. Screening asymptomatic adults for T2D: **ADDITION trial null** for all-cause mortality (HR 1.06). Useful for *deciding* on statins, not an outcome in itself

claims.text: **Screening/test HYPE ‚Äî no mortality evidence** (flagged T4; not independently verified this session)
claims.effect: Multi-cancer early-detection blood tests (Galleri/GRAIL) & whole-body-MRI "executive physical" scans: **no mortality RCT exists**, high false-positive/incidentaloma/overdiagnosis cascade risk. The general principle (more screening ‚âÝ better) is supported by #4‚Äì#5; specific debunk un-sourced here

medical-screening.dose:
**The 80/20 here is "a short list of high-yield prevention, done ‚Äî not a long list of scans, done maximally."** The value concentrates in a handful of RCT-backed items; most of the "more medicine" reflex (extra scans, routine aspirin, pill-stacking) adds harm or cost without extending life.

- **The genuinely life-extending core:** treat **high blood pressure** (the clearest all-cause win, esp. <85yr), take a **statin if your baseline CVD risk is meaningful** (benefit scales with risk ‚Äî high-yield in higher-risk, marginal in low-risk), get **Shingrix** (‚â•50), **RSV** (‚â•60‚Äì75) and seasonal **flu/COVID** vaccines (for morbidity/hospitalization, not as a mortality play), and do **age-appropriate colorectal + cervical (+ lung if a heavy smoker)** screening.
- **Screening reality check:** most cancer screens reduce *dying of that cancer* modestly but have **not** been shown to extend overall life (competing risks, small absolute numbers, screening harms). This is a reason to do the high-value ones and **not** to chase maximal/whole-body screening.
- **Where "less is more":** skip **routine aspirin** (reversed), skip **whole-body MRI / MCED blood tests** (no outcome evidence, incidentaloma cascades), and in older age **deprescribe** (polypharmacy is a real harm ‚Äî see open questions).
- **Risk tools ‚âÝ treatments:** CAC and diabetes screening help *decide* whether to treat (esp. statin decisions), but the test itself doesn't save lives ‚Äî the *treatment* does.
medical-screening.actions:
- [ ] **Know and treat your blood pressure** ‚Äî the clearest causal life-extender in this lever (target individualized; intensive control helps under ~80‚Äì85 but watch for hypotension/kidney effects).
- [ ] **Get your CVD risk assessed and take a statin if risk is meaningful** ‚Äî benefit scales with baseline risk; don't refuse over "statin intolerance" myths (largely nocebo). Consider a CAC score to refine a borderline statin decision.
- [ ] **Get the high-value adult vaccines:** Shingrix (‚â•50), RSV (‚â•60‚Äì75), annual flu + COVID. (Pneumococcal per age/risk ‚Äî protects against disease, not mortality.)
- [ ] **Do age-appropriate cancer screening that has the best benefit-harm ratio:** colorectal (start ~45) and cervical (HPV-based); lung LDCT **only** if a heavy current/former smoker. Understand these mostly prevent *cancer death*, not necessarily *earlier overall death*.
- [ ] **Don't** take daily aspirin for primary prevention, get whole-body MRI / multi-cancer blood tests, or accumulate medications without review. Periodically **deprescribe** with a clinician.
- [ ] Keep a **baseline physician relationship** to enact the above ‚Äî but treat "more tests" with healthy skepticism.
medical-screening.caveats:
- **Disease-specific ‚âÝ all-cause mortality.** The lever's central lesson: a screen/vaccine/drug can reduce deaths from *one* cause without measurably extending overall life. Cancer screens (#1‚Äì#4) reduce cancer deaths modestly but rarely show all-cause benefit; BP/statin treatment (#6, #8) is where the all-cause RCT evidence actually is.
- **Benefit scales with baseline risk** for the drugs ‚Äî statins/BP are high-yield in higher-risk (and older-but-not-very-old) people, marginal in genuinely low-risk young adults. Personalize; don't treat a 30-year-old like a 65-year-old.
- **Healthy-user/healthy-vaccinee confounding** inflates observational estimates here just as in nutrition ‚Äî most starkly for flu-vaccine all-cause mortality (#13). Trust RCTs over administrative-database cohort claims.
- **Overdiagnosis is a real harm**, not a technicality ‚Äî screen-detected DCIS and indolent prostate cancer lead to treatment of disease that would never have caused symptoms (#5). More sensitive tests (MCED, whole-body MRI) amplify this.
- **Age cutoffs matter:** BP benefit fades at 85+; screening benefit requires enough life expectancy to realize it (don't start screens late in life with <10yr expectancy); RSV/Shingrix are age-targeted.
- **This lever is mostly "n/a" on reversibility** ‚Äî it's about *preventing/catching* disease, not a state that reverses. The relevant axis is benefit-vs-harm and absolute magnitude.
medical-screening.openQuestions:
*(also copied to `synthesis/open-questions.md`)*
- **COVID-19 vaccine mortality benefit** ‚Äî crowded out of verification; the large pandemic-era severe-disease/mortality benefit is well-established but **not independently verified in project tiers** this session. (Included in actions on established external consensus, flagged.)
- **Polypharmacy & deprescribing** ‚Äî the "less is more" arm produced no surviving claims; the observational polypharmacy‚Üífalls/mortality association (confounded by indication) and what deprescribing RCTs show on hard endpoints remain unquantified here. Genuinely useful gap for older-adult guidance.
- **MCED (Galleri) / whole-body MRI** ‚Äî no verified outcome data (that's the point: no mortality RCT exists yet; NHS-Galleri pending). Quantified false-positive/overdiagnosis harms would sharpen the #15 debunk.
- **Prostate PSA overdiagnosis** specific rate (~20‚Äì50% indolent) wasn't verified; only the non-significant 37-day lifetime-gain is documented.
- **Where exactly does baseline risk make statins stop extending life?** The threshold below which primary-prevention statins offer negligible absolute benefit wasn't pinned.

nutrition-metabolic.name: Nutrition & Metabolic
nutrition-metabolic.scope:
Diet pattern, protein, visceral fat, glucose, lipids, blood pressure. The hub connecting cardio/neuro/renal decline ‚Äî and the lever most plagued by healthy-user confounding and nutrition-science hype.

- In: dietary PATTERNS (Mediterranean, DASH, plant-forward, Western/ultra-processed); specific FOODS & macronutrients (red & processed meat, added sugar/SSBs, sodium, fiber, fish/omega-3, whole grains); PROTEIN adequacy & the protein/mTOR/IGF-1 longevity debate; COFFEE/caffeine (from substances); CALORIC restriction / intermittent fasting / TRE; body WEIGHT / visceral adiposity / BMI vs mortality; GLYCEMIC / metabolic-syndrome / lipids / BP as modifiable endpoints.
- Out (and where it lives instead): protein/calories to fuel resistance training ‚Üí `exercise`; ALCOHOL ‚Üí `substances` (coffee comes here); longevity-hype supplements (NMN, resveratrol, metformin) ‚Üí T3/T4 holding pen in `synthesis`; PHARMACOLOGIC weight loss (GLP-1) & lipid/BP drugs ‚Üí `medical-screening`; sleep‚Üíappetite ‚Üí `sleep`; dental erosion from sugar ‚Üí `oral-sensory`.

claims.text: Diet QUALITY/PATTERN matters, but the effect shrinks sharply observational‚Üírealistic-increment, and no pattern has an RCT on mortality
claims.effect: Mediterranean high-vs-low all-cause **RR 0.79** (21% ‚Üì) but only **RR 0.92 per 1-SD** (8% ‚Üì ‚Äî the realistic increment). All observational, self-reported at baseline, healthy-user confounded
claims.confidence: H (assoc); the causal magnitude is the open question

claims.text: Plant-based diet QUALITY (not plant-based *label*) drives the association ‚Äî unhealthy-plant-based is *worse*
claims.effect: Healthy plant index (hPDI) all-cause HR **0.86**, CVD 0.83; general PDI 0.85; **UNHEALTHY plant index (uPDI) all-cause 1.20** (‚Üë). ~978k participants, replicated

claims.text: Vegetarian/vegan mortality evidence is mixed and confounded, with a real stroke-harm offset
claims.effect: AHS-2 all-cause HR 0.88 (sig in men 0.82, NS women); **EPIC-Oxford NULL** (veg 1.00, vegan 1.14). Vegetarian CVD RR 0.85, IHD 0.79 ‚Äî but total stroke **+20%**, hemorrhagic **+43%** (survives adjustment)

claims.text: **Ultra-processed food**: dose-response mortality association + a genuine *causal* intake/weight signal
claims.effect: Obs: all-cause **HR 1.15 per 10%** energy from UPF (highest-vs-lowest 1.21), CVD 1.50, **cancer 1.00 (null)**; T2D HR 1.12/10% (1.46 Q5-v-Q1). **[RCT] Hall 2019** metabolic-ward: UPF *caused* **+508 kcal/day** intake & +0.9 kg/2wk (n=20). Obs confounded (UPF tracks smoking/low-SES)
claims.confidence: H (RCT intake signal); M (obs hard endpoints)

claims.text: **Processed meat**: consistent mortality/cancer association; processed > unprocessed red
claims.effect: Processed all-cause **RR 1.15/serving**, CVD 1.15, cancer 1.08. IARC **Group 1** carcinogen, **+18% colorectal/50g** (but small *absolute* risk; ~34k deaths/yr globally vs ~1M tobacco). Unprocessed red weaker, US-only (EPIC null)

claims.text: **Sugar-sweetened beverages**: the most mechanistically-plausible single-food harm (liquid calories, glycemic load)
claims.effect: Per serving/day: **T2D RR 1.27**, CHD 1.20, CVD 1.09, all-cause 1.08‚Äì1.10 (CI ~1.0); ~23% of effect adiposity-mediated. In diabetics, high SSB all-cause HR 1.20

claims.text: **[RCT] DASH diet lowers blood pressure** ‚Äî the cleanest causal diet‚Üíhard-surrogate win
claims.effect: DASH reduced systolic **‚àí6.74 mmHg** (‚àí8.25,‚àí5.23), diastolic **‚àí3.54** (17 RCTs, n=2,561); larger in hypertensives. BP is a validated CVD surrogate ‚Äî this is the strongest *causal* node in the lever

claims.text: **Dietary fiber** is the strongest single-food-group longevity *association* ‚Äî but observational only
claims.effect: High-vs-low **15‚Äì30% lower** all-cause/CVD mortality + 16‚Äì24% lower CHD/stroke/T2D/colorectal-cancer incidence (Reynolds Lancet, 185 cohorts). GRADE moderate. RCTs confirm only *surrogates* (weight, BP, cholesterol). Cereal fiber drives the whole-grain benefit

claims.text: **Fish-vs-fish-oil** is the canonical observational-RCT divergence ‚Äî supplements largely fail
claims.effect: Marine omega-3 **supplements**: all-cause mortality **null (RR ~0.97)**; small CVD-death 0.93, MI 0.92; and a dose-dependent **+25% atrial-fibrillation risk** (HR 1.25). REDUCE-IT (high-dose EPA, MACE HR 0.75) is an outlier clouded by a non-inert mineral-oil placebo & contradicted by the null STRENGTH trial

claims.text: **Coffee**: robust U-shaped mortality association, nadir ~3‚Äì4 cups/day ‚Äî but confounded, no causal proof
claims.effect: All-cause nadir ~3.5 cups **RR 0.85**; benefit present for **decaf too** (so not caffeine alone). **[MR] null** ‚Äî genetically-predicted coffee shows no IHD/stroke/mortality effect ‚Üí the U-curve is largely confounded (reverse causation: sick people quit). No hard-endpoint RCT

claims.text: **Body weight / BMI**: a J-curve with the nadir at ~20‚Äì25, and a *causally-cleaner* signal than most of nutrition
claims.effect: Never-smokers, early deaths excluded: overweight (25‚Äì30) HR **1.13**, obesity-1 (30‚Äì35) **1.44**, obesity-2 (35‚Äì40) **1.88**, severe (40+) **2.51**; **+31% per 5 BMI units** over 25. Underweight also raised (HR ~1.5). ~1.5‚Äì10.6M participants

claims.text: The **"obesity paradox" is a reverse-causation/collider artifact**, not protection from excess weight
claims.effect: The apparent "overweight protective" finding (crude HR ~0.95) *reverses* when restricting to never-smokers & excluding early follow-up (smoking + illness-driven weight loss are the confounders). Longer follow-up *strengthens* the obesity association
claims.confidence: H (qualitative); specific reversal magnitudes were refuted

claims.text: **Central/visceral adiposity** independently predicts mortality beyond BMI
claims.effect: Waist circumference & waist-to-hip ratio each **~HR 1.15 per SD** for CVD death (BMI no longer independent after full adjustment); high WC raises risk **even within normal BMI** (HR ~1.61). Waist matters beyond the scale

claims.text: **Adequate protein favours muscle preservation**; the protein-restriction-for-longevity story is the weaker bet
claims.effect: Pooled 32 cohorts (715k): total protein all-cause HR **0.94**, **plant** protein 0.92 (CVD 0.88), **animal protein null** (1.00) ‚Üí food-source/healthy-user confounding, not protein per se. Adequate protein (~1.0‚Äì1.2 g/kg) for sarcopenia has stronger human support than mTOR/IGF-1 restriction (rodent/mechanistic)
claims.confidence: H (plant assoc); the restriction-for-longevity case is T3

claims.text: **[RCT] Intentional weight loss did NOT cut hard CV events** ‚Äî the causal counterpoint to #11
claims.tier: T1 RCT, but a *null* (placed here as the key caveat to the adiposity association)
claims.effect: **Look AHEAD** (n=5,145 T2D, intensive lifestyle): sustained 6‚Äì8.6% weight loss yet primary CV composite **HR 0.95 (NS)**, stopped for futility. The strong BMI *association* (#11) does NOT translate to a demonstrated causal CV benefit from intentional weight loss (in diabetics). Post-hoc ‚â•10%-loss benefit is non-randomized

claims.text: **Fasting / caloric restriction / TRE: no human longevity evidence ‚Äî surrogate-only, mostly rodent extrapolation**
claims.tier: T2/T3 (RCT on surrogates; longevity claim is animal)
claims.effect: CALERIE-2 (only sustained-CR RCT in non-obese humans, ~12% CR): cardiometabolic markers improve but **no hard endpoints**, and **telomeres were null/slightly worse** (opposite the rodent story). Intermittent fasting does **NOT** beat plain calorie restriction (‚àí0.41 kg, NS); time-restricted eating **null in TREAT** (~65% of lost weight was lean mass) ‚Äî benefit is entirely calorie-mediated
claims.confidence: M (the deflation is solid; longevity claims are T3)

claims.text: The Levine/Longo "high protein in midlife shortens lifespan" claim is one small confounded cohort ‚Äî contested
claims.effect: Levine 2014: high protein at 50‚Äì65 ‚Üí all-cause HR 1.74, cancer 4.33√ó ‚Äî but from a **single 24-hr recall**, sparse-data-unstable (diabetes HR 73!), and the effect **vanished adjusting for animal-protein source**. The "protective after 65" companion was refuted; a 2025 re-analysis found no association. Don't treat as established

claims.text: Calibration: the red-meat GRADE controversy ‚Äî the certainty of evidence is genuinely LOW
claims.effect: 2019 NutriRECS/Annals GRADE review rated the meat-mortality certainty **LOW** and issued a *weak* recommendation to *continue* current intake (~8‚Äì9 fewer deaths/1000 over 11y). A reminder that single-food relative risks rest on low-certainty observational data

claims.text: Refuted / overturned specifics ‚Äî do not cite
claims.effect: All failed verification: a clean fiber "plateau at 25‚Äì29 g/day"; whole-grain "monotonic dose-response"; per-3%-energy plant-protein substitution figures; specific obesity-paradox reversal magnitudes (HR 1.16/1.51); WHtR U-shape nadir HRs. Use the conservative figures in #1‚Äì#14

nutrition-metabolic.dose:
**The 80/20 for nutrition is "don't eat badly," not "eat superfoods."** The robust, replicated, mechanistically-plausible signals are the *harms* (ultra-processed food, SSBs, excess processed meat) and *adiposity*; the "beneficial food" signals are real but observational, confounded, and small once you account for healthy-user effects.

- **The highest-value moves** are subtractive and weight-related: avoid ultra-processed food and sugar-sweetened beverages (the one food with a causal intake/weight RCT, #4), keep processed meat low (#5), and **keep BMI in the ~20‚Äì25 range / waist in check** (#11, #13 ‚Äî the causally-cleanest signal, though intentional weight loss isn't proven on hard endpoints, #15).
- **Pattern > individual foods:** a Mediterranean / high-fiber / plant-*forward* (not necessarily vegetarian) pattern captures most of the benefit; DASH is the one pattern with a clean causal (BP) win (#7). Plant-based done badly (refined grains, sugar) is *worse*, not better (#2).
- **Fiber:** aim for a high-fiber diet (most-vs-least ‚âà 15‚Äì30% lower mortality association), but there's no proven magic threshold ‚Äî the "25‚Äì29 g plateau" was refuted (#19). More plants, less refining.
- **Skip the hype:** omega-3 *supplements* don't reproduce fish-the-food and raise AF risk (#9); coffee is fine and probably net-positive but not a proven lever (#10); fasting/CR/TRE buy nothing beyond the calorie reduction they cause (#16); protein-restriction-for-longevity is rodent-grade ‚Äî favour *adequate* protein for muscle (#14).
- **Protein dose:** ~1.0‚Äì1.2 g/kg for an older adult (muscle preservation) is the better-supported target than restriction; plant protein is the cleaner association but largely a food-source/healthy-user effect.
nutrition-metabolic.actions:
- [ ] **Minimize ultra-processed food and sugar-sweetened beverages** ‚Äî the most defensible dietary harms (UPF has the one causal weight-gain RCT; SSBs have the clearest mechanism).
- [ ] **Keep BMI ~20‚Äì25 and waist in check** ‚Äî adiposity is the causally-cleanest nutrition signal once reverse causation is stripped out. (Caveat: *intentional* weight loss isn't proven to cut hard CV events, so frame this as "don't gain," not "weight-loss is a guaranteed fix.")
- [ ] **Eat a plant-forward, high-fiber, Mediterranean-style pattern** ‚Äî captures most of the benefit; you don't need to be vegetarian (and badly-done plant-based is worse).
- [ ] **Keep processed meat low**; unprocessed red meat is a weaker/low-certainty signal ‚Äî moderate, don't agonize.
- [ ] **Get adequate protein (~1.0‚Äì1.2 g/kg), favouring plant + lean sources** ‚Äî for muscle preservation; ignore protein-restriction-for-longevity claims.
- [ ] **Don't chase supplements or fasting protocols** ‚Äî fish oil doesn't replace fish (and raises AF risk); fasting/CR/TRE add nothing beyond calorie control. Coffee (‚â§3‚Äì4 cups) is fine.
- [ ] If hypertensive, **adopt DASH** ‚Äî the one diet with proven causal BP lowering (~‚àí7/‚àí4 mmHg).
nutrition-metabolic.caveats:
- **Healthy-user confounding is the dominant, pervasive threat** ‚Äî flagged for every observational row. Diet-quality adherers and plant-eaters are systematically leaner, more active, non-smoking, higher-SES; authors of every cohort acknowledge residual confounding. The big associations are real but weak *levers*.
- **Three different failure modes, same lesson:** (a) patterns/foods ‚Üí *self-selection confounding* (effect shrinks observational‚ÜíRCT); (b) omega-3 ‚Üí *food-vs-supplement gap* (reductionist nutrients don't reproduce whole-food signals); (c) fasting/CR ‚Üí *rodent over-extrapolation* (no human hard-endpoint data). All argue against superfood/supplement/protocol optimization.
- **Adiposity is the exception that's causally cleaner** ‚Äî large pooled cohorts with reverse-causation handled by exclusion (never-smokers, drop early deaths) give a robust J-curve. BUT the one intentional-weight-loss RCT on hard endpoints (Look AHEAD) was null, so "lose weight ‚Üí live longer" is association, not proven causation. Best read: *avoid gaining* excess adiposity; weight-loss benefit on hard endpoints is genuinely uncertain.
- **Reversibility is SLOW, not irreversible** ‚Äî cardiometabolic markers, weight, BP, lipids recover over weeks‚Äìmonths to years; no "banking," but also no permanent damage from a period of poor diet (except where it already triggered atherosclerosis/cancer, which live in those systems). DASH-BP is the fastest-responding node.
- **Absolute vs relative risk:** several scary relative risks (processed meat +18% colorectal) are small in *absolute* terms; the GRADE certainty for single-food claims is genuinely low (#18). Don't over-weight a single food.
- **Population fit:** the protein/sarcopenia guidance is most relevant for *older* adults; the BMI nadir and curves are from large multi-continent cohorts (mostly mitigated for smoking/illness confounding).
nutrition-metabolic.openQuestions:
*(also copied to `synthesis/open-questions.md`)*
- **PREDIMED** (the Mediterranean-diet RCT, primary MACE HR ~0.69‚Äì0.72 in high-risk adults) was crowded out of verification across runs and is **not independently confirmed here** ‚Äî the best-known diet RCT remains a gap; its well-known result is consistent with #1/#7 but treat as not-yet-verified-in-project-tiers. (DASH-BP #7 already anchors the causal-diet story.)
- **Sodium ‚Üí hard endpoints / the PURE J-curve** ‚Äî DASH/sodium lower BP (surrogate), but whether sodium *reduction* cuts hard CVD events, and the contested very-low-sodium-harm (PURE) signal, were not verified. Added-sugar (beyond SSBs) specifics also un-pinned.
- **Does the Look AHEAD null generalize beyond diabetics?** No RCT of intentional weight loss powered for hard endpoints exists in a non-diabetic population ‚Äî so the causal benefit of weight loss on mortality is genuinely untested outside diabetes.
- **Any human causal evidence for protein-restriction/mTOR longevity?** Currently entirely rodent/mechanistic (T3). And the muscle-preservation side (~1.0‚Äì1.2 g/kg) was asserted as stronger but its specific RCT magnitudes weren't in the confirmed set.
- **Fiber dose-response shape** ‚Äî the clean "25‚Äì29 g plateau" was refuted; is benefit monotonic-with-attenuation or is there a true ceiling?

oral-sensory.name: Oral & Sensory
oral-sensory.scope:
Teeth/enamel/gums, hearing, vision. **The "irreversible hardware" lever** ‚Äî cochlear hair cells, retinal/optic neurons, tooth enamel, and alveolar bone don't regenerate, so prevention & timely correction dominate. Hearing loss is the single largest *modifiable* dementia risk factor.

- In: age-related HEARING loss & hearing aids/amplification (and the dementia/falls/depression links); VISION ‚Äî cataract, age-related macular degeneration, glaucoma, uncorrected refractive error (and the dementia/falls/mortality links); ORAL health ‚Äî periodontal disease, tooth loss/edentulism (and the systemic CVD/mortality/dementia associations); the IRREVERSIBILITY framing and where prevention (noise protection, correction, dental hygiene) is the only lever.
- Out (and where it lives instead): oral-CANCER screening & tobacco‚Üíoral-cancer mortality ‚Üí `substances` / `medical-screening`; dental erosion driven by sugar/SSBs ‚Üí mechanism noted here but `nutrition-metabolic` owns sugar; diabetic retinopathy management ‚Üí `medical-screening`/`metabolic`; smell/taste loss (minor ‚Äî note only); cataract *surgery* as a procedure ‚Üí the dementia/falls *outcome* stays here, surgical logistics out.

claims.text: **Hearing loss** is the largest single *modifiable* dementia risk factor ‚Äî strong dose-response association
claims.effect: Incident dementia: mild HL HR **1.89**, moderate 3.00, severe 4.94; **+27% per 10 dB** (log-linear). Meta (14 cohorts) HR 1.59 ‚Üí 1.45 (pub-bias corrected); AD-specific 2.24. Survived 2‚Äì6yr washout (partial defense vs reverse causation)

claims.text: Lancet Commission: hearing loss ~8% of dementia PAF ‚Äî but newer cohorts suggest this is **inflated**
claims.effect: 2020 Commission weighted **PAF 8.2%** (largest single factor; 12 factors = ~40%). BUT UK Biobank HR only **1.12** (PAF ~4.7%), Whitehall NS ‚Äî older estimates likely overstated. Real effect modest

claims.text: **[RCT] ACHIEVE** ‚Äî hearing aids slow cognitive decline in HIGHER-RISK older adults, but not in healthy volunteers
claims.effect: Overall (n=977): **NULL** (Œî 0.002 SD, p=0.96). But higher-risk ARIC subgroup (n=238): **48% slower** 3-yr decline (p=0.027); top risk-quartile **58‚Äì62% slower**. Interaction p=0.01. The causal test: works where risk is high, not in the worried-well

claims.text: Hearing-aid use is observationally associated with less cognitive decline & depression ‚Äî but healthy-user confounded
claims.effect: Hearing-aid use the largest single protective factor for episodic memory (Œ≤ 1.53) in one survey; dementia attenuated in aid-users. Consistent with #3's direction but confounded (aid-users are healthier, higher-SES)

claims.text: **Cochlear hair-cell loss is IRREVERSIBLE** in humans ‚Äî prevention (noise) + early correction is the only lever
claims.effect: Adult human cochlear hair cells do not regenerate ‚Üí noise-induced & age-related hearing loss is **permanent**. The lever is (a) prevent noise damage, (b) correct early with amplification

claims.text: **Vision impairment** is associated with incident dementia ‚Äî but causality is narrow (MR subtype-specific only)
claims.effect: Vision impairment ‚Üí dementia HR **1.43‚Äì1.55** (UK Biobank 1.55, 31-study meta 1.43). Cross-sectional PAF up to 19% (explicit *upper bound*) vs Lancet Commission 2024 ~2%. MR supports only a *subtype* (non-AD non-vascular OR 1.62), not all-cause/AD

claims.text: Vision impairment ‚Üí higher all-cause mortality, with a severity gradient
claims.effect: All-cause mortality HR **1.29** (15 cohorts, I¬≤=31%); gradient: moderate 1.43, severe/blindness 1.89. Best-corrected (vs presenting) acuity stronger ‚Üí non-refractive pathology/shared risk factors contribute. Reverse causation live (vision loss as frailty marker)

claims.text: **Cataract surgery** is the strongest near-causal sensory lever ‚Äî vision *restoration* tracks lower dementia
claims.effect: ~**25% lower** dementia (RR 0.74‚Äì0.75, pooled ~450‚Äì558k). Lee 2022 HR **0.71** with an elegant negative control: **glaucoma surgery** (doesn't restore vision) was null (1.08) ‚Üí restoration, not surgery/healthy-user effects, drives it. CONTESTED: a 2024 meta found all-cause result NS (HR 0.85, fragile); no RCT exists

claims.text: Reversible (cataract, refractive error) vs IRREVERSIBLE (AMD, glaucoma, diabetic retinopathy) vision loss
claims.effect: Cataract & uncorrected refractive error are the **treatable** causes (glasses, surgery) ‚Äî the actionable lever. AMD/glaucoma/DR cause **irreversible** retinal-ganglion/photoreceptor loss (no regeneration); AMD is the leading irreversible blindness in older adults
claims.reversibility: **mixed** ‚Äî correct the reversible, prevent the rest

claims.text: **Periodontitis / tooth loss** is associated with mortality & CVD ‚Äî but the magnitudes scream confounding
claims.effect: Periodontitis all-cause mortality RR **1.46**; edentulism 1.66; edentulism CHD mortality RR **2.98** ‚Äî *far* larger than plausible for a causal oral‚Üíheart effect. Smoking/diabetes/low-SES cause both ‚Üí residual confounding inflates these

claims.text: Tooth loss ‚Üí dementia is a weak, geographically-inconsistent signal
claims.effect: Tooth loss ‚Üí dementia RR **1.15**, cognitive decline 1.20 ‚Äî but driven by Asian studies, **NS in Europe (1.06) / North America (1.03)** for dementia (I¬≤=67%). Reverse causation live (early dementia ‚Üí neglected oral care)

claims.text: **[MR] Mendelian randomization finds NO causal effect** of periodontitis on stroke, CAD, Alzheimer's, or endocarditis
claims.effect: Periodontitis MR: ischaemic stroke OR 1.00, CAD 1.01, carotid-IMT Œ≤ ~0, AD OR 1.00 (p=0.31), endocarditis null. **The observational associations (#10‚Äì#11) are confounding, not causation.** Reverse causation (AD ‚Üí poor oral hygiene) remains plausible

claims.text: Periodontal-treatment RCTs move only SURROGATES ‚Äî never hard endpoints
claims.effect: Intensive treatment: 24h systolic BP **‚àí11 mmHg** (n=101, 2mo, authors say "proof of concept"); HbA1c **‚àí0.64%** (3mo) attenuating to ‚àí0.33% (6mo). **NO RCT** shows periodontal treatment cuts CV events, dementia, or mortality

claims.text: The genuinely actionable core for oral health is **masticatory FUNCTION**, not systemic-disease prevention
claims.effect: Tooth loss causally impairs chewing ‚Üí dietary adaptation & **malnutrition RR ~1.10‚Äì1.22**. Tellingly, **denture use neutralizes the dementia association** (no-denture RR 1.70 ‚Üí with-denture 1.04) ‚Äî restored *function*, not teeth per se, is the plausible mediator. Keep functional teeth for nutrition/QoL
claims.reversibility: tooth loss IRREVERSIBLE; dentures restore function

claims.text: The **P. gingivalis ‚Üí Alzheimer's** hypothesis is mechanistic hype ‚Äî the trial FAILED
claims.effect: Oral-bacteria-cause-AD theory (gingipains in AD brains) ‚Üí the Cortexyme **GAIN/atuzaginstat phase 2/3 trial FAILED** its primary cognitive endpoint. Don't treat "fix your gums to prevent dementia" as established

claims.text: PAF estimates for sensory‚Üídementia are inflated/contested
claims.effect: Hearing PAF 8% (2020) ‚Üí ~4.7% (newer UK cohorts); vision cross-sectional PAF "19%" is an explicit upper bound vs Lancet ~2%. The *direction* is robust; the *magnitude* is smaller than headlines

claims.text: Refuted specifics ‚Äî do not cite
claims.effect: All failed verification: APOE-Œµ4 as the dominant confounder of cataract‚Üídementia; "DR is the leading global cause of blindness"; an MR claim of periodontitis *causally* raising BP; "shared risk factors are the explicit conclusion" framings. Use the conservative claims above

oral-sensory.dose:
**The 80/20: prevent the permanent damage, and correct the *treatable* sensory losses early ‚Äî especially if you're already at cognitive risk.** This lever has little "dose" and a lot of "fix it before it compounds."

- **Hearing:** protect against noise (the damage is irreversible), and **get hearing aids early if you have loss** ‚Äî the ACHIEVE RCT shows real cognitive benefit *in higher-risk older adults* (the people most likely to be reading this later in life), even though it didn't help healthy volunteers. Don't wait years to address hearing loss.
- **Vision:** correct refractive error (glasses) and **get cataracts removed** when they impair vision ‚Äî the near-causal evidence (vision-restoration ‚Üí lower dementia, glaucoma-surgery negative control) is the best in this lever, even if not RCT-proven. AMD/glaucoma/DR are irreversible, so the lever there is prevention/early detection (overlaps medical-screening).
- **Oral:** keep your teeth via basic hygiene and regular dental care ‚Äî but for the **right reason**: *chewing function and nutrition*, not because gum disease causes heart attacks or dementia (MR says it doesn't). If you do lose teeth, **use dentures** ‚Äî restored function appears to matter.
- **What NOT to believe:** "treat your gums to prevent heart disease/Alzheimer's." The treatment RCTs move only surrogates; MR is null; the P. gingivalis trial failed.
oral-sensory.actions:
- [ ] **Protect your hearing from loud noise** (the damage is permanent) ‚Äî earplugs at concerts/power tools, volume limits.
- [ ] **Get hearing tested and use hearing aids early if you have loss** ‚Äî strongest cognitive benefit in those already at risk (ACHIEVE). Don't normalize years of untreated loss.
- [ ] **Correct vision: wear your glasses, and get cataract surgery when cataracts impair you** ‚Äî the best near-causal cognition lever here; also reduces falls.
- [ ] **Keep functional teeth** (brush/floss, regular dental care) for chewing & nutrition; if you lose teeth, **get and wear dentures**.
- [ ] **Don't** buy the "gum disease causes heart disease/dementia, so periodontal treatment will prevent them" pitch ‚Äî keep your gums healthy for your *mouth*, and rely on the actual CV/dementia levers elsewhere.
- [ ] Treat sensory correction as **cognitive-reserve maintenance**, especially from midlife on ‚Äî these are among the few *modifiable* dementia levers.
oral-sensory.caveats:
- **Irreversibility is the defining axis.** Cochlear hair cells, retinal/optic neurons, and tooth enamel/alveolar bone don't regenerate ‚Äî so unlike most levers, "recovery" isn't available; the value is in *prevention* and *compensation* (aids, surgery, dentures, glasses). This is why early action matters more here than dose.
- **Causal evidence is thin and the probes narrow it.** The associations are large but mostly observational with heavy confounding/reverse causation. The three causal probes all *deflate*: ACHIEVE is null overall (benefit only in high-risk), cataract‚Üídementia is contested with no RCT, and periodontitis‚Üísystemic is **MR-null**. Trust the probes over the raw HRs.
- **Reverse causation is everywhere:** early dementia reduces auditory/visual engagement and oral self-care, so part of every sensory‚Üídementia association runs backward. Washout analyses (hearing) and negative controls (cataract) partially address it but don't eliminate it.
- **Benefit concentrates in the already-at-risk** (ACHIEVE's lesson) and in *midlife-onward* ‚Äî these are dementia-prevention levers most relevant as you age, less so for a healthy young adult.
- **Oral health's defensible rationale is function/nutrition** (chewing, malnutrition, QoL), which is real and independent of the debunked systemic-disease story ‚Äî but even that evidence is mostly cross-sectional.
- **Magnitudes are inflated in popular framing:** the "hearing loss is 8% of dementia" / "vision 19%" figures are upper bounds; newer data put them lower. The direction is solid; don't over-rank on the headline PAF.
oral-sensory.openQuestions:
*(also copied to `synthesis/open-questions.md`)*
- **No RCT of cataract surgery or refractive correction on dementia/falls/mortality** exists ‚Äî the vision lever rests entirely on observational + negative-control inference. Would an RCT replicate the ~25% signal, and why do the 2024 meta-analyses conflict (RR 0.74 vs 0.85 NS)?
- **Falls** ‚Äî vision (and hearing) impairment plausibly raise falls (a mortality pathway) but no hazard ratio survived verification; the falls-prevention *intervention* evidence lives in the exercise lever (balance training).
- **Which PAF is right for ranking** ‚Äî hearing 8% vs ~5% (newer); vision 19% (cross-sectional) vs 2% (Lancet)? The magnitude materially affects how high these rank.
- **Does treating hearing loss reduce dementia *incidence* (not just slow cognitive-test decline)** over longer follow-up, and beyond the high-risk subgroup? ACHIEVE was 3 years on a cognitive-test surrogate.
- **Periodontal treatment hard-endpoint RCT** ‚Äî none exists; the surrogate effects (BP, HbA1c) are real but attenuate. Would a large/long trial show any hard-endpoint benefit, or is the MR-null the final word?

sleep.name: Sleep
sleep.scope:
Duration, consolidation, circadian alignment, recovery. The downstream-of-everything lever ‚Äî and the one most entangled with reverse causation (illness disrupts sleep as much as sleep disrupts health).

- In: sleep duration (the U-shaped mortality curve); sleep quality / consolidation / efficiency / fragmentation; circadian alignment & timing (shift work, social jetlag, sleep regularity, chronotype); major sleep disorders as mortality/CVD drivers (insomnia, obstructive sleep apnea); napping; behavioral sleep hygiene (light exposure, screens, timing); and INTERVENTION evidence ‚Äî does improving sleep (CBT-I, treating OSA/CPAP, sleep extension) change hard endpoints?
- Out (and where it lives instead): exercise-as-recovery / training adaptation ‚Üí `exercise`; caffeine & alcohol *as substances* ‚Üí `substances` (their sleep-disruption mechanism is noted here, but the mortality claim stays there); sleep's metabolic/glucose markers (insulin sensitivity, weight) ‚Üí `nutrition-metabolic` (the CVD/mortality endpoint stays here); depression/anxiety as drivers of insomnia ‚Üí `stress-social` (bidirectional ‚Äî flagged, not owned here); dementia mechanism deep-dive (glymphatic/amyloid) ‚Üí ticked in `neurological`, but the sleep‚Üídementia risk claim stays here; melatonin/pharmacologic sleep aids ‚Üí basic sleep-aid evidence noted here, deep supplement analysis deferred.

claims.text: Sleep duration vs all-cause mortality is U/J-shaped, nadir ~7‚Äì7.5h ‚Äî but the two arms are not equal
claims.effect: Short <7h all-cause HR **1.14** (1.10‚Äì1.18); long ‚â•9h HR **1.34** (1.26‚Äì1.42). Single large cohort (NHANES) steeper: ‚â§5h 1.28 AC / 1.32 CV; ‚â•9h 1.53 AC / 1.74 CV; spline nadir 7.04‚Äì7.32h

claims.text: The long-sleep arm is largely a REVERSE-CAUSATION artifact, and the two biggest meta-analyses disagree on short sleep
claims.effect: Long sleep I¬≤=92%, publication bias (Egger p=0.02); authors tie it to "undiagnosed illness, depression, low SES." Kwok 2018 (74 cohorts, 3.34M): J-shaped, long sleep drives risk (RR 1.14@9h‚Üí1.47@11h) but short <7h **NS**; MR finds **no causal** long-sleep‚Üístroke effect

claims.text: The same U-shape holds for CVD, cerebrovascular disease, and stroke (not just mortality), nadir ~7.5h
claims.effect: CVD nadir ~7.5h (low-risk 4.3‚Äì10.3h); cerebrovascular ~7.5h (p=0.05, marginal). Stroke: short ‚â§5‚Äì6h HR 1.29 incidence / 1.12 mortality; long >8‚Äì9h 1.46 / 1.45 (long arm reverse-causal)

claims.text: Insomnia is a MODEST predictor of cardiovascular events and a WEAK one for mortality
claims.effect: Insomnia‚ÜíCVD RR **1.31** (1.14‚Äì1.51); ‚ÜíMI 1.48 (1.03‚Äì2.12, fragile, I¬≤=84%); ‚Üíall-cause mortality only **1.14**, significant only at 10‚Äì20yr follow-up. Poor sleep *quality* ‚Üí CHD RR 1.44 but **not** mortality. (A "CV-mortality RR 1.53" claim was **refuted** ‚Äî see #16)

claims.text: Sleep-timing IRREGULARITY (objective accelerometry) is a strong mortality predictor ‚Äî the best-measured sleep signal
claims.effect: Low Sleep Regularity Index (5th pct vs median), UK Biobank n=88,975: all-cause HR 1.53 ‚Üí **1.22** (1.07‚Äì1.39) fully adjusted; CVD-mortality **1.88** (largest); cancer 1.36 (prodromal ‚Äî early-follow-up HR >2 falls to ~1.05 by yr 4)

claims.text: **[MR]** The strongest CAUSAL thread: genetically-predicted insomnia raises coronary artery disease and hypertension risk
claims.effect: Genetic insomnia ‚Üí CAD OR **1.14** (1.10‚Äì1.19); ‚Üí hypertension OR **1.16** (1.13‚Äì1.18). Germline instruments are immune to reverse causation. Caveat: authors hedge to "possibly causal"; CAD I¬≤=97% (HTN more robust, I¬≤=28%)

claims.text: Untreated moderate‚Äìsevere OSA is a substantial CVD/mortality risk ‚Äî OBSERVATIONALLY
claims.effect: AHI dose-response: moderate (15‚Äì30) HR 1.56, severe (>30) **2.45**. Pooled: composite CV HR 1.82, stroke 2.12, CV-mortality 1.89, all-cause mortality **1.68**

claims.text: **[RCT]** ‚Ä¶but CPAP does NOT reduce hard cardiovascular events ‚Äî the central observational-vs-RCT divergence
claims.effect: Pooled 6 RCTs (n=4,493, established CVD): MACE OR **0.94** (0.79‚Äì1.12, NS). Null driven by poor adherence (SAVE 3.3 h/night, ISAACC 2.78 ‚Äî below the ~4h where benefit appears). The ">4h benefit" is post-hoc, healthy-adherer-biased. CPAP DOES improve sleepiness, BP (~2‚Äì3 mmHg), QoL ‚Äî **not** hard CV events

claims.text: Sleep‚Üídementia is mostly REVERSE CAUSATION (prodromal), not a large causal lever
claims.effect: Short sleep *strengthens* with lag (OR 1.44@6yr ‚Üí 3.44@19yr ‚Äî argues against pure reverse causation); long sleep prodromal (sig only ~3yr). **MR null**: duration‚Üíall-cause dementia OR 1.19 (NS), ‚ÜíAD 0.89 (NS). AD's ~20yr prodrome biases observational sleep‚ÄìAD links

claims.text: Glymphatic/slow-wave amyloid clearance: real human single-night signals, but surrogate-only
claims.effect: One night total deprivation raised amyloid-PET ~**5%** (SUVr 1.35‚Üí1.42, n=20); slow-wave disruption raised CSF AŒ≤40. Small, single-night, CSF/PET/plasma surrogates ‚Äî **no link to dementia incidence**, nothing showing better sleep prevents AD

claims.text: **[RCT]** CBT-I reliably treats insomnia itself ‚Äî first-line, durable to ~1 year
claims.effect: ISI Hedges g **0.64** (3mo) ‚Üí 0.40 (6mo) ‚Üí 0.25 (12mo); older adults: sleep efficiency +8.4 pp, SOL ‚àí9.3 min, WASO ‚àí23.4 min; digital CBT-I SMD ‚àí0.71 (29 RCTs, n=9,475). AASM first-line over hypnotics (conditional, low-GRADE). Endpoint is the *surrogate* (insomnia severity), not a hard outcome

claims.text: **[RCT]** The single strongest CAUSAL win: CBT-I PREVENTS major depression ‚Äî a hard endpoint
claims.effect: Older adults (n=291, mean 70), CBT-I vs active comparator, 36-mo: incident/recurrent DSM-5 depression 12.2% vs 25.9%, HR **0.51** (0.29‚Äì0.88, ~49% ‚Üì), **NNT 7.3**. (Responder-subgroup HR 0.17 is real but its *mediation* framing was refuted ‚Äî cite the ITT 0.51; see #16)

claims.text: **[RCT]** CBT-I lowers inflammation (CRP) ‚Äî but thin, single-lab, surrogate
claims.effect: Irwin n=123: CBT-I reduced CRP (overall p=.04) + genome-wide proinflammatory expression. Single lab (UCLA), not independently replicated for CBT-I; Tai Chi Chih showed *more durable* cellular effects ‚Äî so the anti-inflammatory benefit may not be sleep-specific

claims.text: **[RCT]** Sleep EXTENSION in short sleepers cuts appetite and a little weight ‚Äî small, short, surrogate
claims.effect: Habitual <6.5h sleepers extended ~1.2h/night (n=80, 2 wk): energy intake **‚àí270 kcal/day** (objective, doubly-labeled water); weight ‚àí0.87 kg ‚Äî but **mostly fat-free mass** (‚àí0.62 kg lean vs ‚àí0.25 kg fat). Insulin/HOMA-IR claim **refuted** (#17). No BP or hard-endpoint signal

claims.text: Reversibility of sleep loss ‚Äî a genuine EVIDENCE GAP this session
claims.effect: No claim on acute-debt recovery, "sleep banking," weekend catch-up, or chronic-restriction normalization survived adversarial verification. Working tiers (physiology, NOT verified here): acute debt ‚âà **FAST** (largely recoverable, imperfectly); chronic-restriction risk ‚âà **SLOW** to normalize; "sleep banking" weak. Treat as unresolved

claims.text: The CBT-I "sustained remission ‚Üí 82.6% depression reduction (HR 0.17)" MEDIATION framing was REFUTED (0‚Äì3)
claims.effect: The responder-subgroup number is real but it's per-protocol, not ITT; the causal-mediation interpretation didn't survive verification. The defensible causal figure is the ITT **HR 0.51** (#12)

claims.text: Refuted / overturned magnitudes ‚Äî do not cite
claims.effect: All failed verification: insomnia ‚Üí CV-mortality "RR 1.53"; sleep-extension ‚Üí insulin/HOMA-IR improvement; "long sleep causally raises stroke" (MR null). Use the conservative figures in #1‚Äì#14

claims.text: The "sleep clears amyloid ‚Üí prevents Alzheimer's" causal story is mechanistic hype, not outcome evidence
claims.effect: The glymphatic narrative rests on single-night surrogate shifts (#10) and mouse work; **no human evidence** that improving sleep lowers dementia incidence, and MR is null (#9). Plausible mechanism ‚âÝ actionable longevity lever

sleep.dose:
**The 80/20 is unusual for this lever: the dose target is real but the *causal payoff* of hitting it is smaller than the scary HRs imply.**

- **Duration:** aim for **~7‚Äì7.5h** ‚Äî the consistent nadir for mortality, CVD, cerebrovascular disease, and stroke. But two honest caveats: (a) the *long-sleep* danger zone (‚â•9h) is mostly sick people sleeping more, not sleep harming you ‚Äî don't fear-set an alarm to cap sleep; (b) the two biggest meta-analyses don't even agree that *short* sleep (<7h) raises mortality. Treat 7‚Äì7.5h as a sensible target, not a knife-edge.
- **Regularity beats duration heroics:** the best-measured signal is *irregular* timing (objective SRI ‚Üí CVD-mortality HR 1.88). Consistent sleep/wake times are likely higher-yield than chasing an exact duration. (Still observational.)
- **Insomnia / quality:** the actionable move is **CBT-I**, not sleeping pills ‚Äî it's first-line, durable ~1 year, and (the real prize) roughly halves incident depression. This is the one place "fixing sleep" has hard-endpoint causal evidence.
- **OSA:** if you have moderate‚Äìsevere OSA, treat it for **symptoms** (sleepiness, BP, quality of life) ‚Äî but do **not** expect CPAP to prevent heart attacks; the RCTs are null. Adherence <4h/night is the norm and part of why.
- **Where the lever is NOT:** chasing specific sleep *stages*, glymphatic "brain detox," or wearable scores has no hard-outcome evidence. Don't optimize the surrogate.
sleep.actions:
- [ ] Target a **regular ~7‚Äì7.5h** sleep opportunity, with consistent sleep/wake times (regularity may matter more than the exact number).
- [ ] Don't catastrophize occasional short nights or naturally longer sleep ‚Äî the long-sleep "risk" is mostly reverse causation, and acute debt is largely recoverable.
- [ ] If you have chronic insomnia, do **CBT-I** (in person or a validated digital program) ‚Äî first-line, durable, and the one sleep intervention with a hard causal payoff (‚âà49% lower incident depression). Avoid hypnotics as the default.
- [ ] If you snore heavily / have witnessed apneas / daytime sleepiness, get screened for **OSA** and treat it ‚Äî for *symptoms and BP*, not as a heart-attack-prevention strategy. If you use CPAP, aim for >4h/night or it does little.
- [ ] Protect sleep as a *depression and daytime-function* lever (its best-evidenced benefits), not as a longevity silver bullet ‚Äî the mortality HRs overstate the causal stakes.
sleep.caveats:
- **Causality is the whole story.** Of ~62 confirmed claims, the genuinely causal ones are narrow: MR (insomnia‚ÜíCAD/HTN, #6) and two RCT threads (CBT-I‚Üídepression #12; CPAP‚Üí*no* MACE #8). Everything else is observational with **reverse causation as a live, author-acknowledged confounder** ‚Äî illness, depression, and low SES cause poor/long sleep, inflating every HR. The large numbers are real *associations* but weak *levers*.
- **The OSA paradox is the teaching case:** strong observational risk (mortality HR up to 2.45) + null CPAP RCTs (MACE OR 0.94). Either the risk is confounded, or CPAP adherence is too poor to realize it ‚Äî probably both. Don't let the observational number set the expectation for treatment benefit.
- **Dementia link is mostly prodromal:** sleep changes appear years before diagnosis (AD has a ~20-yr prodrome), MR is null, and the glymphatic story is surrogate-only. Sleep is more *early-warning sign* than *cause* of dementia.
- **CBT-I evidence is strong for insomnia/depression, thinner downstream:** the depression result is one large single-site RCT in *older* adults (does it generalize to mid-life?); the inflammation/CRP benefit is one single-lab RCT and may not be sleep-specific (Tai Chi did better). No RCT shows CBT-I or sleep extension lowering CV events or mortality.
- **Sleep extension** trials are small (n=80), short (2 wk), and surrogate; the weight loss was mostly *fat-free* mass (not clearly good), and the insulin-sensitivity claim was refuted. Promising for appetite, not established for cardiometabolic health.
- **Reversibility is genuinely unresolved here** (#15) ‚Äî physiologically acute debt is largely FAST-recoverable and chronic risk likely SLOW-normalizing, but no verified claim pins it. Don't assert "sleep banking" or "weekend catch-up erases the risk."
- **Most cohort data is self-reported** (recall bias); the SRI/UK Biobank study (objective accelerometry) is the notable, stronger exception. Several duration meta-analyses share overlapping author groups (reduced independence).
sleep.openQuestions:
*(also copied to `synthesis/open-questions.md`)*
- **Reversibility tiers (FAST/SLOW/IRREVERSIBLE)** ‚Äî acute-debt full recovery vs persistent deficits, "sleep banking" (Rupp/Wesensten), weekend catch-up offsetting weekday risk, and chronic-restriction normalization. None survived verification ‚Äî the one part of the question still genuinely open. A dedicated pass could close it, but it's low decision-relevance (the actions don't change).
- **Does CBT-I prevent depression in non-elderly adults?** The hard-endpoint RCT is older-adults, single-site. Generalization + multi-site replication unknown.
- **Any RCT evidence that improving sleep lowers hard CV events / BP / mortality?** The confirmed set has none (CBT-I and sleep extension only touch surrogates/depression). True gap vs out-of-scope-search ‚Äî unclear.
- **Is the CBT-I anti-inflammatory effect sleep-specific?** Tai Chi Chih produced more durable cellular effects than CBT-I in the same trial ‚Äî suggests the inflammation benefit may be a general behavioral-intervention effect, not sleep per se.
- **Short-sleep causality** ‚Äî the two biggest meta-analyses disagree on whether <7h raises mortality at all; MR-insomnia suggests *some* causal cardiometabolic effect, but the duration-specific causal magnitude is unresolved.

stress-social.name: Stress & Social
stress-social.scope:
Chronic stress, loneliness, social connection, sense of purpose. Mortality effect sizes rival physical risk factors.

substances.name: Substances
substances.scope:
Tobacco, alcohol, other recreational/psychoactive substances. The single largest modifiable mortality cluster.

- In: cigarette smoking (incl. low-intensity), smokeless tobacco, alcohol, nicotine vaping (attempted), cannabis (attempted).
- Out (and where it lives instead): caffeine ‚Üí `nutrition-metabolic`; substance use as a stress-coping mechanism / social drinking dynamics ‚Üí `stress-social`; medication/polypharmacy ‚Üí `medical-screening`; oral cancer screening & dental effects of tobacco ‚Üí ticked in `oral-sensory` but the mortality claim stays here.

claims.text: Lifelong cigarette smoking roughly triples all-cause mortality
claims.effect: HR ~2.7‚Äì3.0 vs never-smokers (Jha 3.0‚ôÄ/2.8‚ôÇ; Cho 2.8‚ôÄ/2.7‚ôÇ; Inoue-Choi exclusive-current 2.23)
claims.reversibility: SLOW (mortality risk falls after quitting; some damage IRREVERSIBLE ‚Äî see #8)

claims.text: Lifelong smoking costs ~10‚Äì13 years of life expectancy
claims.effect: ‚àí10 to ‚àí13 yrs vs never-smokers (Cho: 12‚ôÄ/13‚ôÇ yrs lost over ages 40‚Äì79)

claims.text: No risk-free smoking intensity: even <1 cig/day raises mortality
claims.effect: <1 CPD HR 1.64 (CI 1.07‚Äì2.51); 1‚Äì10 CPD HR 1.87 (1.64‚Äì2.13). 1‚Äì10 CPD cause-specific: lung cancer HR 11.6, respiratory 6.0, CVD 1.50

claims.text: Quitting before age 40 averts ~90% of smoking's excess mortality
claims.effect: Pre-40 cessation cuts excess death ~90% (Jha); residual HR ~1.2 vs 3.0 continued. Cho: 95%‚ôÄ/90%‚ôÇ under-40; 81/61% at 40‚Äì49; 63/54% at 50‚Äì59

claims.text: Cessation benefit emerges fast ‚Äî detectable ~3 years after quitting
claims.effect: Benefit evident by 3 yrs post-cessation; ‚â•10 yrs quit ‚Üí survival ‚âà never-smoker (Cho)

claims.effect: Former smokers HR ~1.33 (1.28‚Äì1.37) vs never-users ‚Äî far below current 2.23, above 1.0

claims.text: Smokeless tobacco: real but much smaller all-cause penalty than smoking
claims.effect: Exclusive current SLT all-cause HR 1.36 (1.17‚Äì1.59) vs never-tobacco

claims.text: **Vaping** vs NEVER-use: modest composite-CVD signal; MI & stroke not significant

claims.effect: Mittleman 2001 (n=3,882 MI), route-specific to smoking, wide CI; pooled *chronic* estimates NS. An acute-trigger story, not chronic risk

claims.effect: Alimoradi 2025, 14 cohorts: RR 1.53 (1.09‚Äì2.14) but I¬≤=98%, unadjusted, author-graded LOW. The "doubles mortality" (RR 2.53 general-pop split) framing was REFUTED in our verification
